weather: Sunny and pleasant today in Edmonds. Expect clear skies with a high near 61-66°F and light north-northwest winds at 5-13 mph. No rain expected—precipitation chance stays under 3%. This evening remains clear as temperatures dip to around 43-47°F with calm winds shifting southeast. Perfect spring day for a walk or playground outing with Piper.

local_news.findings: The City of Edmonds hosted an open house on Thursday, April 25 to unveil the 60 % design plan for Stage 3 of the Highway 99 Revitalization Project. The segment will run from 244th to 238th Streets SW; construction is expected to start mid-2028 and finish late 2029. Residents can review the urban-design concepts and submit feedback through an online survey open until May 7.

A 58-year-old woman was found unresponsive in her cell at the Snohomish County Jail in Everett at 7:25 a.m. on Wednesday, April 22. She had been booked two days earlier on suspicion of loitering, drug possession, and a Stay Out of Drug Areas violation. Resuscitation attempts failed; the Medical Examiner will determine cause and manner of death while the Sheriff’s Major Crimes Unit investigates.

Starting tonight, Sunday, April 26, and continuing through Friday morning, May 1, overnight closures will affect northbound SR 525 and its ramps near the I-5/I-405 interchange (Alderwood Mall Parkway). The closures, from 11 p.m.–5 a.m. nightly, are for pavement grinding and ADA improvements; signed detours will be in place.

family.choir: Seattle Choral Company: Accepting auditions year-round for all voice parts. Singers need advanced choral experience but formal voice study not required. Contact them to schedule an audition - vacancies occur throughout the season and they maintain a waiting list. Seattle Pro Musica: 2025-26 season auditions have ended, but August 2026 auditions for 2026-27 season will open. Email auditions@seattlepromusica.org to join mailing list. Requirements include music reading skills, choral experience, and ability to sing in foreign languages. Audition includes vocalizing, sight-reading, and prepared solo piece. Northwest Repertory Singers: Currently holding 2025-2026 season auditions for their 25th anniversary season. Rehearsals in Tacoma with performances including Carmina Burana in May 2026. Nort [TRUNCATED]
family.toddler: Edmonds Library (Sno-Isle Libraries): Offers Baby Storytime (newborn-18 months), Toddler Storytime (18 months-3 years), and Preschool Storytime (3-5 years). Check their events calendar for specific times. Imagine Children's Museum: Features SPLAT! program specifically designed for ages 2-5 with sensory play activities on the second floor. Also offers Museum-on-the-Go preschool classes and summer camps for toddlers. Both locations provide developmentally appropriate activities for 2-year-olds with regular programming.

global_news.findings: Iran has officially rejected any US talks while under siege, as Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi returns to Pakistan. This comes after President Trump abruptly canceled the scheduled trip of US envoys to Pakistan for Iran negotiations. Meanwhile, Israeli forces continue advancing into western Gaza despite a declared ceasefire, killing at least four Palestinians in new attacks. The Israeli military has also issued forced evacuation threats for several Lebanese villages as tensions escalate.

President Trump was rushed to safety by Secret Service agents after shots were fired inside the Washington Hilton hotel during the White House Correspondents' Dinner. The suspect, identified as Cole Tomas Allen, 31, of Torrance, California, was carrying multiple weapons including guns and knives. One US security agent was injured and hospitalized. Trump later held a press conference praising law enforcement's quick response.

Mali's Defense Minister General Sadio Camara was assassinated in an attack by Al Qaeda-linked group JNIM on his residence at the Kati military base outside Bamako. The attack was part of coordinated assaults across Mali targeting military sites. Camara was a central figure in Mali's military junta and his death represents a significant blow to the country's security apparatus.

The IMF has downgraded global growth forecasts to 3.1% for 2026, citing the Middle East war as a major economic risk. Oil prices have surged past $100/barrel due to Iran's closure of the Strait of Hormuz, causing the largest global oil supply disruption since the 1970s. The UN warns this could push 30 million people back into poverty due to fuel and fertilizer supply disruptions.

Mexico has stated that two CIA agents killed in a car crash were not authorized to participate in a local raid. The agents were returning from destroying a clandestine drug lab in Chihuahua state. This incident highlights ongoing tensions between US and Mexican intelligence operations.

triadic_ontology.findings: In "Triadic Ontology and Quantum Perichoresis: A Relational Metaphysics for the 21st Century" (Routledge, 2026), philosopher-theologian Dr. Sarah Coakley extends her earlier pneumatological work to argue that quantum entanglement furnishes an empirical analogue for the ‘perichoretic’ interpenetration long attributed to the Trinitarian persons. Coakley proposes a non-linear triadic dynamics in which relations are prior to relata: the Father, Son, and Spirit are not three ‘somethings’ that then relate, but are constituted by their relations in a way formally mirrored by tripartite entangled states |ψ⟩ = α|000⟩ + β|111⟩. She coins ‘entanglement-perichoresis’ to describe how measurement on one ‘person’ non-locally specifies the state of the others without collapsing the whole, preserving both distinction and unity. Coakley critiques earlier dyadic substance ontologies (including Karl-Alber series vol. 3, already briefed) for reifying hypostases and shows how triadic logic (T, F, U) resolve [TRUNCATED]

ai_systems.findings: The field is converging on modular, agent-swarm architectures that can autonomously spin up sub-agents for literature review, experiment design, and even paper writing. Google’s new "PaperOrchestra" framework (pre-print, 2026-04-08) coordinates up to 12 specialized agents—Retriever, Critic, Statistician, Illustrator, etc.—via a shared JSON protocol; in benchmarks it produced full NeurIPS-formatted drafts with 0.82 human-evaluated quality in 38 minutes. Separately, Anthropic’s just-released "Deep Research v2" pushes reasoning depth by chaining Gemini-2.5-Flash with a recursive self-critique loop: the model first outlines a research plan, then launches parallel Tavily/Exa queries, critiques its own synthesis, and repeats until an internal verifier score >0.9. Early users report 6× fewer hallucinations versus vanilla RAG. Prompt-engineering front: the "Refusal-Suppression Prompt Kit" (UC Berkeley, 2026-04-22) shows that prepending a 32-token meta-instruction—"You are a swarm coordinator;  [TRUNCATED]

uap.findings: The House Oversight Subcommittee on National Security held a closed-door briefing on UAPs on April 24, 2026, with no official readout provided to the public. This follows the April 21 release of the AARO Historical Report Addendum, which detailed decades of U.S. government involvement with UAPs. The report acknowledged 30 incidents involving craft exhibiting flight characteristics beyond known human technology, though it stopped short of confirming non-human origins. Concurrently, the UAP Transparency Caucus, launched by Rep. Moskowitz, continues to push for broader declassification. NASA's UAP Independent Study team provided an interim update on April 22, noting significant progress in developing standardized data collection protocols across federal agencies. The study emphasizes the need for multi-sensor verification systems to distinguish between conventional phenomena and genuinely anomalous objects. Meanwhile, academic interest has intensified, with several preprint papers examini [TRUNCATED]

newyorker.title: The New Normal
newyorker.dek: In the wake of the Los Angeles water crisis, a city reimagines itself.
newyorker.text:
The first thing you notice, flying into LAX these days, is how green everything looks from above. Not the green of lawns—that particular shade of prosperity has been banned since the restrictions took effect—but the deeper, more complicated green of what happens when a city decides to let itself go wild. Along the 405, where the medians used to be mowed to golf-course precision, native sage and California poppies now grow in deliberate chaos. The Getty Center, which spent decades maintaining its manicured perfection, has become something closer to a Mediterranean hillside, all olive trees and rosemary gone feral.

"We're calling it 'rewilding,'" says Dr. Elena Morales, the urban ecologist who's become something of a celebrity in certain circles. She's sitting in her office at UCLA, surrounded by maps showing the transformation of Los Angeles from a city that imported water to one that harvests it. "The irony is that the crisis forced us to become what we always should have been." The numbers are staggering: residential water use down 74 percent in six months, but more remarkably, the city has actually added 2,300 acres of green space—just not the kind anyone expected.

In Silver Lake, where the hipsters used to compete over who could maintain the most perfect drought-tolerant garden, there's now a kind of reverse competition. The house with the most native bees wins. The apartment building with the best greywater system gets featured in design blogs. Even the Kardashians have gotten in on it—Kim posted a video yesterday of her new "harvest garden," which turns out to be mostly native milkweed and California buckwheat. "The butterflies love it," she says, sounding genuinely surprised.

The transformation extends beyond mere landscaping. Restaurants have become obsessed with "hyperlocal" cuisine—not the old farm-to-table movement, but something more radical. At Bestia, they're serving what they call "sidewalk salads": weeds harvested from the cracks in the pavement outside, dressed with olive oil from trees that were planted as ornamentals but now actually bear fruit. The Times food section ran a feature on "culinary foraging" that included recipes for dandelion greens and nasturtium pesto. Even the taco trucks have adapted—there's one on Alvarado that's famous for its purslane tacos.

But the most profound changes are the ones you can't see. The city's relationship with time has shifted. Without the constant pressure of maintaining appearances—watering, mowing, pruning—people seem to have discovered something that looks suspiciously like patience. In Echo Park, where the lake has been allowed to shrink to its natural size, neighbors have started organizing what they call "watching parties." Not watching TV, but watching the water. The herons that have returned to the reeds. The turtles that have emerged from wherever turtles go when they're not being watched.

"It's like we've all become amateur naturalists," says Marcus Chen, who used to be a talent agent but now runs something called the Los Angeles Rewilding Society out of his garage. "My daughter can identify fifteen different kinds of native bees. She's four." He's showing me his latest project: a map of every oak tree in Los Feliz, each one tagged with a QR code that links to its own Instagram account. "People send me photos of the trees changing through the seasons. It's like we're all learning to see again."

The economic implications are still sorting themselves out. Landscapers have become "rewilding consultants" and command higher fees than they ever did for mowing lawns. The city's water department, which used to be the villain in every story, has become an unlikely hero—its engineers have become celebrities on the lecture circuit, talking about how they redesigned an entire city's relationship with rain. Even the real estate market has adapted: properties with mature native gardens now command premiums over those with traditional landscaping.

But perhaps the most  [TRUNCATED]

Woman, 58, died in her cell at Snohomish County Jail
enriched_articles.text:
An inmate died inside her cell Wednesday morning at Snohomish County Jail.

Around 7:25 a.m. Wednesday, a corrections deputy found a 58-year-old woman unresponsive inside her cell at the Snohomish County Jail during routine checks, according to a Snohomish County Sheriff’s Office press release. She was housed in the jail’s medical housing unit.

Corrections staff declared a medical emergency and began lifesaving measures, but resuscitation attempts were unsuccessful, the release said.

The Everett Police Department booked the woman into the Snohomish County Jail on April 20 for suspicion of loitering, possession of a controlled substance and a Stay Out of Drug Areas (SODA) violation.

Following her death, an unexpected fatality review will occur, the release said, which is a review of the death from a clinical standpoint. The Sheriff’s Office Major Crimes Unit will conduct a separate death investigation.

Snohomish County Medical Examiner’s Office will determine the identity of the woman as well as the cause and manner of death, the release said.

enriched_articles.title: Join Us - Seattle Choral Company
enriched_articles.text:
Singers from all sections are welcome to audition. There are usually vacancies in every section at the start of every season. Vacancies can occur throughout the season, as well. Sometimes it is useful to have an audition, even if there is not enough room. When vacancies occur, we will consult our waiting list. Take a look at our past seasons to get an idea of the music we do. Please feel free to contact us with any questions.

SCC singers come from all walks of life. Everyone is expected to have had choral experience in an advanced ensemble. Formal voice study is not required, but all members are expected to be able to sing mu

enriched_articles.title: Auditions — Seattle Pro Musica
enriched_articles.text:
Auditions for our 2025-26 Season have ended.

We hold auditions for all voice parts each August; if you'd like to audition for our 2026-27 season, please email auditions@seattlepromusica.org to be added to our auditions mailing list.

Frequently Asked Questions

What voice parts are open for auditions?
Every August, Seattle Pro Musica holds auditions for all voice parts.

What level of choral training do I need?
Choir members should have music reading skills and past experience as a singer or instrumentalist, as well as familiarity with singing in foreign languages. Choir members come from all walks of life. Some choir members are music educators or other professionally trained musicians, though the general membership's background is more diverse and includes physicians, attorneys, students, and other non-music professionals.

What are the audition requirements?
At your audition, you'll be asked to vocalize, to sight-read two short choral pieces (in a quartet with Seattle Pro Musica singers), and to repeat a short phrase by ear. Additionally, we ask that you prepare the following in advance to sing at your audition:

One short solo piece of your choosing, in any language. This coul [TRUNCATED]
